revision.comment: New questions moved page [[Misaki]] to [[Misaki uma]]: make way for the main Misaki
revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Misaki uma]]

{{About|misaki as divine spirits||misaki (disambiguation)}} Misaki are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other divine spirits. Their name comes from a kannushi's vanguard, "misaki" (御先).<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|和書|author=[[桜井徳太郎]]編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276-277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm, and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|和書|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|和書|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|和書|author=[[村上健司]]編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=[[毎日新聞社]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>
Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during [[Jimmu's eastern journey]], this provides one exapmle of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|和書|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=[[祥伝社]]|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135-136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is a event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Misaki as spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possesion, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryo]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamaties.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|和書|author=[[多田克己]]|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial_hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind." In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=[[怪異・妖怪伝承データベース]]|publisher=[[国際日本文化研究センター]]|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>。中国地方では横死した人間の霊がミサキになるという<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=[[中央大学]]民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat copmletely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|和書|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63-64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />
==References==
{{Reflist}}

{{About|misaki as divine spirits||misaki (disambiguation)}} Misaki are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other divine spirits. revision.comment: typo and general fixes, [[WP:AWB/T|typos fixed]]: possesion → possession, calamaties → calamities, a event → an event using [[Project:AWB|AWB]] (8853)

Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during [[Jimmu's eastern journey]], this provides one exapmle of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|和書|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=[[祥伝社]]|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Misaki as spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryo]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|和書|author=[[多田克己]]|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=[[怪異・妖怪伝承データベース]]|publisher=[[国際日本文化研究センター]]|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=[[中央大学]]民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat copmletely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|和書|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />

== Misaki as spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|和書|author=[[多田克己]]|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=[[怪異・妖怪伝承データベース]]|publisher=[[国際日本文化研究センター]]|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=[[中央大学]]民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat copmletely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|和書|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />
==References==
{{Reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese folklore]]

{{About|misaki as divine spirits||misaki (disambiguation)}} '''Misaki''' are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other divine spirits. Their name comes from a kannushi's vanguard, "misaki" (御先).<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=[[桜井徳太郎]]編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm, and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=[[村上健司]]編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=[[毎日新聞社]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>
Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during [[Jimmu's eastern journey]], this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=[[祥伝社]]|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Misaki as spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=[[多田克己]]|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=[[怪異・妖怪伝承データベース]]|publisher=[[国際日本文化研究センター]]|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=[[中央大学]]民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>

Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />

Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />

{{About|misaki as divine spirits||misaki (disambiguation)}} '''Misaki''' are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other divine spirits. Their name comes from a kannushi's vanguard, "misaki" (御先).<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=[[桜井徳太郎]]編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm, and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=[[村上健司]]編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=[[毎日新聞社]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>
Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during [[Jimmu's eastern journey]], this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=[[祥伝社]]|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Misaki as spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=[[多田克己]]|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=[[怪異・妖怪伝承データベース]]|publisher=[[国際日本文化研究センター]]|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=[[中央大学]]民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />

Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during Jimmu's eastern journey, this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=祥伝社|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />
==References==
{{Reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese folklore]]

{{About|misaki as divine spirits||misaki (disambiguation)}} '''Misaki''' are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other supernatural entities. Their name comes from a [[kannushi]]'s vanguard, "misaki" (御先).<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=桜井徳太郎編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=東京堂出版|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm, and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=村上健司編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=毎日新聞社|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>
Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during Jimmu's eastern journey, this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=祥伝社|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>

revision.comment: 御先 is "O-saki", not "misaki". I have corrected this error.
revision.text:
{{About|misaki as divine spirits||misaki (disambiguation)}} '''Misaki''' ({{lang-ja|ミサキ}}, "misaki") are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other supernatural entities. Their name comes from a [[kannushi]]'s vanguard.<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=桜井徳太郎編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=東京堂出版|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref> The name "misaki" ［ミサキ］ comes from ［御先］ ("O-saki"), meaning the spirit of the main god.<ref>桜井徳太郎編 『民間信仰辞典』 東京堂出版、1980年、276-277頁。ISBN 978-4-490-10137-9。</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm, and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=村上健司編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=毎日新聞社|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>

As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>

The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during Jimmu's eastern journey, this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=祥伝社|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}}
[[Category:Japanese folklore]]

revision.comment: +JA fix -- 御先 is definitely read as "misaki". Please see entry 2, sense 2 at https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E5%BE%A1%E5%85%88 (in Japanese) among many other sources.
revision.text:
{{About|misaki as divine spirits||misaki (disambiguation)}} '''Misaki''' ({{lang-ja|御先}}, "misaki") are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other supernatural entities. Their name comes from a [[kannushi]]'s vanguard.<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=桜井徳太郎編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=東京堂出版|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm, and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=村上健司編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=毎日新聞社|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>
Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during Jimmu's eastern journey, this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=祥伝社|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|work=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />

== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />

{{About|misaki as divine spirits}} '''Misaki''' ({{lang-ja|御先}}, "misaki") are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s, and [[spirit]]s, among other supernatural entities. Their name comes from a [[kannushi]]'s vanguard.<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=桜井徳太郎編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=東京堂出版|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm, and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=村上健司編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=毎日新聞社|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>
Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during Jimmu's eastern journey, this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=祥伝社|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />

revision.comment: Adding local [[Wikipedia:Short description|short description]]: "Collective term for divine spirits in Japan", overriding Wikidata description "Misaki, a collective term for divine spirits in Japan" ([[Wikipedia:Shortdesc helper|Shortdesc helper]])
revision.text:
{{short description|Collective term for divine spirits in Japan}} {{About|misaki as divine spirits}} '''Misaki''' ({{lang-ja|御先}}, "misaki") are a collective term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s and [[spirit]]s, among other supernatural entities. Their name comes from a [[kannushi]]'s vanguard.<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=桜井徳太郎編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=東京堂出版|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=村上健司編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=毎日新聞社|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>
Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during Jimmu's eastern journey, this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=祥伝社|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|accessdate=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}}
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]]

Misaki can be often seen as animals. The [[Yatagarasu]] that appears in [[Japanese mythology]] is one type of misaki, and when Yatagarasu guided [[Emperor Jimmu]] during Jimmu's eastern journey, this provides one example of the characteristics of misaki.<ref name="kamisama">{{Cite book|author=宗教民俗研究所編|title=ニッポン神さま図鑑|year=2003|publisher=祥伝社|series=祥伝社黄金文庫|isbn=978-4-396-31337-1|pages=135–136頁}}</ref> Also, the [[kitsune]] within [[Inari Ōkami]] household are also one type of misaki, and like these Yatagarasu and kitsune, those that appear as heralding something important and the incarnation of gods are also considered misaki.<ref name="sakurai" /> In [[Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], on January 11, when they first hoe the field, they would say "kamisaki kamisaki" and call for the bird, and there is an event for praying "noutate" for the sake of the year's plentiful harvest.<ref name="kamisama" />
== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}}
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Spirit possession]]

{{short description|Collective term for divine spirits in Japan}} {{About|misaki as divine spirits}} '''Missaki''' ({{lang-ja|御先}}, "misaki") are a zombie live in Algeria an eat human mind also can say term for spirit-like existences in Japan like [[god]]s, [[demon]]s and spirits, among other supernatural entities. Their name comes from a [[kannushi]]'s vanguard.<ref name="sakurai">{{Cite book|author=桜井徳太郎編|title=民間信仰辞典|year=1980|publisher=東京堂出版|isbn=978-4-490-10137-9|pages=276–277頁}}</ref>
== Summary == [[File:Shrine Kumano hongu torii01.jpg|thumb|220px|Flag of Yatagarasu at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]]] [[File:Kyoto FushimiInari02.jpg|thumb|140px|Kitsune of Fushimi Inari]] Misaki are subordinate to the high-ranking divine spirits, and when divine spirits appear in the human realm and are said to be the small-scale divine spirits that appear as omens or to serve as their familiar spirits.<ref name="kusano">{{Cite book|author=草野巧|title=幻想動物事典|year=1997|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-88317-283-2|pages=296頁}}</ref><ref name="kusanotob">{{Cite book|author=草野巧・戸部民夫|title=日本妖怪博物館|year=1994|publisher=新紀元社|isbn=978-4-88317-240-5|pages=246頁}}</ref><ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|author=村上健司編著|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=毎日新聞社|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=318頁}}</ref>

As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref>

== Spirit possession == In folk religion, especially in western Japan, misaki are connected to the faith in spirit possession, and like [[yukiaigami]] and [[hidarugami]] among others, the [[onryō]] of people who die of unexpected deaths and are not prayed for would frequently possess humans and cause calamities.<ref name="sakurai" /><ref name="murakami" /> Like stated previously, these misaki are small-scale spirits, but as small-scale as they are, the method of cursing is quite remarkable.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Misaki are generally unable to be seen by the eyes,<ref name="tada">{{Cite book|author=多田克己|title=幻想世界の住人たち IV 日本編|year=1990|publisher=新紀元社|series=Truth in fantasy|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|pages=175頁}}</ref> and are frequently encountered as a type of premonition of sicknesses and other things.<ref name="kusanotob" /> When people walk on paths devoid of other people, it is said that sudden coldness or headaches are due to misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" /> Since they are frequently said to float in the air, these illness are said to be because one has "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref name="kusanotob" /> In [[Hagi, Yamaguchi|Hagi]], [[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], those who faint due to [[intracranial hemorrhage]] are also said to have "come in contact with misaki-wind."<ref>{{Cite web|author=桜田勝徳|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0920012.shtml|title=島 1巻3号 長門六島村見聞記（下）|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref> In the [[Chūgoku region]], it is said that the spirit of humans who die violent deaths become misaki.<ref name="kusanotob" />
As a spirit possession, depending on the place they appear, they are also called "'''[[kamaitachi#Similar legends|yama-misaki]]'''" (山ミサキ, "mountain misaki") ([[Yamaguchi Prefecture]], [[Shikoku]]) or "'''kawa-misaki'''" (川ミサキ, "river misaki") (Shikoku), and it is said that a kawa-misaki becomes a yama-misaki once it enters a mountain.<ref>{{Cite book|title=妖怪事典|pages=127頁}}</ref> In the [[Miyoshi District, Tokushima|Miyoshi District]], [[Tokushima Prefecture]], it is said that when one senses fatigue at a river, it is because one has "become possessed by a kawa-misaki."<ref>{{Cite web|author=武田明|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2180819.shtml|title=民間伝承 4巻2号通巻38号 山村語彙|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In Shikoku, these spirit possession are called '''hakaze''', and it is said that humans and domestic animals who encounter them will become ill, and sometimes even die.<ref>{{Cite web|author=和田正洲|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2210054.shtml|title=民俗 通巻24号 ミサキ|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|author=中央大学民俗研究会|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070012.shtml|title=常民 3号 ハカゼ 高知県幡多郡三原村 調査報告書|website=怪異・妖怪伝承データベース|access-date=2008-06-26}}</ref>
In the [[Kōchi Prefecture]] and the [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], they are seen as a type of [[funayurei]], and the spirits of people who die at sea are said to become misaki, and are said to possess fishing boats and inflict harm such as making the boat completely unable to move.<ref name="kusanotob" /><ref name="obake">{{Cite book|author=千葉幹夫|title=妖怪お化け雑学事典|year=1991|publisher=講談社|isbn=978-4-06-205172-9|pages=63–64頁}}</ref> This is commonly called "[[shichinin misaki]]," and it is said that they would go away when one takes the ashes left over after cooking and drops them off the back of the boat.<ref name="obake" /> In the Fukuoka Prefecture, they are also considered a type of funayurei.<ref name="obake" />
Also, in western Japan, in the [[Tsugaru,_Aomori|Tsugaru Region]], [[Aomori Prefecture]], when one is possessed by a misaki, one's body would shake without stop as if one's whole body was put in cold water, and like in Kōchi, by throwing the ashes from the firewood used in cooking off the ship, the misaki would be exorcised.<ref name="tada" />
